name: Harlow v. Fitzgerald
summary: White House aides were sued for allegedly conspiring to retaliate against a Pentagon whistleblower by suppressing his congressional testimony. The Court replaced the prior subjective good-faith qualified immunity test with an objective standard based solely on whether the law was clearly established at the time of the allegedly unconstitutional conduct.
holding: Government officials performing discretionary functions are entitled to qualified immunity from civil liability unless their conduct violates clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known at the time of the act. The test is objective, not subjective — the official's actual beliefs or motives are irrelevant; only the state of clearly established law governs the immunity determination.

legal_definitions.definition: A defense available to government officials sued in their individual capacities for civil rights violations, shielding them from damages liability unless their conduct violated clearly established law that a reasonable official would have known. The inquiry is entirely objective — the official's personal beliefs or intentions are irrelevant.
legal_definitions.context: Harlow reformulated qualified immunity to protect all but the plainly incompetent or those who knowingly violate the law, replacing the prior Scheuer v. Rhodes test that included a subjective good-faith inquiry.

relevance_to_audit: Establishes the controlling qualified immunity standard for government officers sued individually; relevant for assessing personal liability exposure of officials who authorize or operate surveillance programs, issue unlawful contracts, or direct constitutional violations.

key_quotes.text: Government officials performing discretionary functions generally are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known.
key_quotes.context: Articulating the objective clearly-established-law standard that replaced the prior subjective good-faith test for qualified immunity.

procedural_posture: D.C. Circuit reversed dismissal; affirmed in part
subsequent_treatment: Governing qualified immunity standard; clarified by Hope v. Pelzer (2002) and Pearson v. Callahan (2009); increasingly criticized by courts and commentators
